passages.title: Cloze Test 17-21
passages.text: (I) The Industrial Revolution marked a major turning point in history, (___) almost every aspect of daily life. (II) It began in Great Britain in the late 18th century and (___) spread to other parts of the world. (III) This period saw the transition from manual labor (___) machine-based manufacturing. (IV) (___) , urban populations exploded as people moved to cities for work. (V) This shift brought about both economic growth and social (___) Question 17:

passages.title: Cloze Test 22-26
passages.text: (I) Photosynthesis is the process (___) green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods. (II) It (___) involves the green pigment chlorophyll and generates oxygen as a byproduct. (III) Without this process, life on Earth (___) as we know it. (IV) Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, (___) helps mitigate the greenhouse effect. (V) (___) , protecting forests is crucial for maintaining planetary health. Question 22:

passages.title: Reading 1
passages.text: The Anthropocene epoch, a proposed geological period dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems, remains a subject of intense debate among stratigraphers. While some argue that the mid-20th century, with its surge in radioactive isotopes from nuclear testing, serves as the definitive starting point, others suggest the Industrial Revolution or even the dawn of agriculture. The primary challenge lies in identifying a 'golden spike'—a physical marker in the rock strata that universally signifies this transition. Regardless of its official designation, the concept underscores the unprecedented scale at which human activity now shapes planetary systems, from carbon cycles to biodiversity loss.

passages.title: Reading 2
passages.text: Behavioral economics challenges the classical assumption of the 'homo economicus'—the perfectly rational actor who always makes decisions to maximize utility. Instead, it incorporates psychological insights to explain why individuals often act irrationally. Concepts such as loss aversion, where the pain of losing is psychologically twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining, and the endowment effect, where people overvalue what they already own, provide a more nuanced understanding of market behavior. These 'heuristics' or mental shortcuts are often efficient but can lead to systematic biases. By understanding these biases, policymakers can design 'nudges' that steer people toward better choices without restricting their freedom. The concept of 'loss aversion' implies that:

passages.title: Reading 3
passages.text: The Library of Alexandria, once the pinnacle of ancient knowledge, was not destroyed in a single cataclysmic event as popular myth suggests. Rather, its decline was a protracted process spanning centuries, caused by a combination of budget cuts, civil unrest, and changing political priorities. While Julius Caesar is often blamed for burning it during his Siege of Alexandria in 48 BC, the library likely survived in some form long after. Later conflicts, including the decree of Theodosius I against pagan temples and the Muslim conquest of Egypt, further contributed to its eventual disappearance. The loss of its scrolls represents one of the greatest cultural tragedies of the ancient world, yet much of its contents had already been lost to neglect before the final blow.

passages.title: Reading 4
passages.text: Epigenetics refers to the study of heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence. Essentially, while the 'blueprint' of the DNA remains the same, external factors—such as diet, stress, and environmental toxins—can trigger chemical modifications that turn genes on or off. One of the most common mechanisms is DNA methylation. These changes can be passed down to future generations, meaning that the lifestyle choices of ancestors could potentially affect the health of their descendants. This field challenges the traditional view that our genetic destiny is fixed at birth, suggesting a dynamic interaction between nature and nurture. Epigenetics is defined as the study of:

passages.title: Reading 5
passages.text: The emergence of Abstract Expressionism in post-World War II New York marked a pivotal shift in the art world, moving its center from Paris to the United States. Artists like Jackson Pollock and Mark Rothko sought to express profound emotional and subconscious states through non-representational forms. Pollock's 'drip paintings' emphasized the physical act of painting itself, a technique known as action painting, while Rothko's 'color fields' utilized large expanses of color to evoke a sense of the sublime. Though criticized by some as chaotic or lacking skill, the movement was a response to the trauma of the war, seeking a new visual language that transcended traditional boundaries. Abstract Expressionism is characterized by:

Librarian: We've noticed a decline in the borrowing of physical books lately.

options.A: We should consider closing the library's physical location then.

options.C: However, many people still prefer the tactile experience of a real book.
options.D: Do you think we should increase the fines for late returns?

Doctor: Your blood pressure is still high despite the medication.

Patient: I didn't realize that. I'll start checking the labels more carefully.

options.A: You should try to exercise at least thirty minutes every day.

options.C: Many processed foods contain hidden sodium that you might not notice.

questions.question:
Journalist: Do you think AI will eventually replace human creative writers? 
Author: I believe AI can mimic style, but it lacks genuine lived experience. 
Journalist: ________ 
Author: Precisely. It can arrange words beautifully, but it doesn't 'feel' the grief or joy it describes.

options.A: So, it lacks the emotional depth that comes from being human?
options.B: Can AI be programmed to understand human emotions better?
options.C: Are you worried that your latest book will be compared to AI work?
options.D: Is there a specific software that you use to help your writing?
options.E: Will AI ever be able to write a full-length novel on its own?

questions.question: The hypothesis was rejected not because the data was inaccurate, but because the methodology was fundamentally flawed.

options.B: The hypothesis was dismissed because the way the study was conducted was incorrect, not due to wrong data.
options.C: Both the data and the methodology were so flawed that the hypothesis had to be rejected.
options.D: The methodology was corrected after the hypothesis was rejected due to inaccurate data.
options.E: If the data had been accurate, the flawed methodology would not have resulted in rejection.

options.B: The new law was supported by most people until civil rights groups started to protest.
options.C: As soon as the new law came into effect, it was strongly challenged by civil rights organizations.
options.D: Because of the fierce opposition, the government decided not to enact the new law.

options.E: Intensive reforestation has already mitigated the effects of soil erosion in the region.

questions.question: The concept of the 'uncanny valley' describes the eerie sensation people feel when they encounter humanoid robots. As a robot becomes more human-like, our emotional response becomes increasingly positive. ________ However, when the resemblance is nearly perfect but slightly 'off,' the response turns into one of strong revulsion.

options.A: This is why most people prefer robots that look like machines.
options.B: The more a robot looks like a human, the more we trust it unconditionally.
options.C: This trend continues until a point where the robot looks almost, but not quite, human.
options.D: Scientists have yet to find a way to make robots look truly human.
options.E: Robots are becoming more common in the healthcare and service industries.

questions.question: Renewable energy sources such as wind and solar are crucial for a low-carbon future. However, their intermittent nature poses a challenge for the stability of the power grid. ________ To address this, researchers are developing advanced battery systems and pumped hydro storage to ensure a steady supply of electricity.

options.A: Electricity prices are expected to drop as technology improves.
options.B: Energy storage is therefore essential to balance supply and demand.
options.C: Solar panels are becoming more efficient every year.
options.D: Nuclear power remains a controversial alternative to renewables.
options.E: Many countries are still dependent on coal for their energy needs.

questions.question: In the world of literature, 'The Great Gatsby' is often cited as the definitive American novel. It explores themes of wealth, class, and the corruption of the American Dream during the Roaring Twenties. ________ Through the character of Jay Gatsby, Fitzgerald critiques a society obsessed with status and material success.

options.A: The novel was not very successful when it was first published.
options.B: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote the book while living in France.
options.C: Its vivid depiction of the jazz age remains unparalleled in fiction.
options.D: Most readers find the plot to be overly complicated and slow.
options.E: The American Dream is a concept that appeals to people worldwide.

questions.question: The Fibonacci sequence is a series of numbers where each number is the sum of the two preceding ones. This mathematical pattern is not just a theoretical curiosity. ________ From the arrangement of leaves on a stem to the spiral of a galaxy, the sequence appears with startling frequency.

options.A: It was first discovered by an Italian mathematician in the 13th century.
options.B: Computers use this sequence to solve complex encryption problems.
options.C: It is found throughout the natural world in various forms.
options.D: Many students find it difficult to understand the logic behind it.
options.E: The sequence starts with zero and one, followed by one, two, and three.

questions.question: (I) Dark matter is an invisible substance that makes up about 27% of the universe. (II) It does not emit, absorb, or reflect light, making it extremely difficult to detect directly. (III) Astronomers observe its presence through its gravitational effects on visible matter. (IV) The James Webb Space Telescope was launched to study the early universe in infrared. (V) Without dark matter, galaxies would likely fly apart rather than rotate as they do.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) The Renaissance was a period of great cultural and intellectual rebirth in Europe. (II) It began in Italy in the 14th century and later spread to the rest of the continent. (III) During this time, artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo created their masterpieces. (IV) The Black Death had previously wiped out a significant portion of the European population. (V) This era also saw significant advancements in science, politics, and philosophy.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants use sunlight to synthesize nutrients. (II) It involves the conversion of carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. (III) Chlorophyll is the pigment responsible for capturing the light energy needed for this reaction. (IV) Some plants have adapted to live in desert environments with very little water. (V) This process is fundamental to life on Earth as it provides the primary source of energy for most organisms.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Globalization has led to increased economic integration and cultural exchange between nations. (II) It has allowed for the rapid spread of technology and information across borders. (III) However, it has also been criticized for contributing to wealth inequality and the loss of local traditions. (IV) Many people now travel abroad for vacation more frequently than in the past. (V) Proponents argue that it promotes peace by making countries more dependent on one another.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is a common form of talk therapy used to treat anxiety and depression. (II) It focuses on identifying and changing negative thought patterns and behaviors. (III) Many therapists also use mindfulness techniques to help patients stay present. (IV) Sigmund Freud was the founder of psychoanalysis, which explores the unconscious mind. (V) CBT is generally considered to be a short-term, goal-oriented treatment approach.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V
